item.title: Brazil’s Long-Awaited Interest Rate Cuts to Start With a Whimper
item.description: Brazil’s central bank is widely expected to begin easing monetary policy with a modest interest-rate cut, as war in the Middle East stokes inflation fears and complicates President Luiz Inacio Lula da Silva’s reelection bid.

item.title: Micron Earnings Face High Expectations as Its Stock Keeps Rising
item.description: Soaring prices for memory chips have made Micron Technology Inc. one of the standout stocks of 2026, and its earnings after the close on Wednesday will give clues on whether the rally is sustainable.

item.title: UniCredit CEO Says Commerzbank Bid Intended to Break ‘Stalemate’
item.description: UniCredit SpA Chief Executive Officer Andrea Orcel said his takeover bid for Commerzbank AG is designed to bring about negotiations between the stakeholders as he seeks to breathe fresh life into a deal he’s been pursuing for more than 18 months.

item.title: Citi Sees BOK Raising Rate Toward 3% This Year on Inflation Risk
item.description: The Bank of Korea is likely to raise its policy rate to 3% this year as a surge in global oil prices drives inflation higher, according to Citigroup Inc.

item.title: Tencent’s Sales Rise 13% in Boost for Broader AI Ambitions
item.description: Tencent Holdings Ltd. posted a 13% rise in quarterly revenue, underscoring solid momentum in its core gaming and advertising units while it ramps up a bet on agentic AI.

item.title: Garuda Indonesia Loss Widens as Scheduled Airline Revenue Slips
item.description: Flag carrier PT Garuda Indonesia posted a wider net loss last year as revenue from scheduled airline services weakened and costs in some areas rose.

item.title: Iceland Reverses Course With Quarter-Point Rate Hike to 7.5%
item.description: Iceland’s central bank raised its benchmark interest rate as the first western European monetary authority to tighten policy this year due to growing concerns that the war in the Middle East will keep price pressures high.

item.title: Iran Moves Its Own Oil Through Hormuz as It Chokes Other Traffic
item.description: Iran has been moving its crude through the Strait of Hormuz at rates broadly comparable to transit before the war began, making the most of its control of the vital waterway as other exporters falter.

item.title: UniCredit Sees Up to €500 Million in AI Cost Cuts in Next Years
item.description: UniCredit SpA Chief Executive Officer Andrea Orcel said the bank is quantifying the impact of artificial intelligence at about €400 million to €500 million ($576 million) in net cost reductions over roughly five years, driven by efficiency gains across core processes such as lending, compliance and client onboarding.

item.title: Oil, Gas Majors Cut Green Spending for First Time Since 2017
item.description: The world’s largest oil and gas companies ratcheted back investment in the energy transition in 2025, marking the first annual decline in eight years, according to BloombergNEF.

item.title: UBS Nears Full Credit Suisse Integration by Finishing IT Project
item.description: UBS Group AG has completed the migration of all former Credit Suisse clients to its own systems, marking a substantial step toward finishing the integration of the competitor it bought in a historic deal three years ago.

item.title: South Africa Inflation Slowed in February Before War Impact Felt
item.description: South African inflation cooled in February, but the central bank is unlikely to view this as a reason to cut interest rates next week as it weighs the impact of conflict in the Middle East.

item.title: War in Iran Is Redrawing the Map for Natural Gas
item.description: It's more than just an oil shock.

item.title: Odd Lots: Iran War Redraws the Map for Natural Gas (Podcast)
item.description: Mostly, the world has been watching the price of oil skyrocket amid the war in Iran and the de facto closure of the Strait of Hormuz. But there’s more than just oil that comes out of the region. Qatar is home to the world’s largest natural gas field, and for now, it’s been almost completely cut off from the rest of the world. Not only has Gulf gas supply been cut off, there’s also damage to the core infrastructure, which will take time to repair. Meanwhile, the US is rapidly becoming a natural g

item.title: Samsung Agrees to Supply Next-Generation AI Memory to AMD
item.description: Samsung Electronics Co. agreed to supply and collaborate with Advanced Micro Devices Inc. on next-generation AI memory and computing technologies.

item.title: BOE Assumptions Suggest Hawkish Tilt on UK Rates, Economists Say
item.description: The Bank of England may respond more aggressively than expected to soaring energy prices since war broke out in the Middle East because its internal models assume a large and long-lasting impact on inflation.

item.title: Pepkor Rethinks Investec Tie-Up as It Eyes Solo Bank Startup
item.description: Pepkor Holdings Ltd. is weighing plans to abandon a possible partnership with Investec Bank Ltd. and instead launch a lender on its own, according to a person familiar with the matter.

item.title: Rayner Says Labour ‘Running Out of Time’ in Critique of Starmer
item.description: Angela Rayner, the former deputy prime minister who’s bookmakers’ favorite to succeed Keir Starmer as Labour leader, said the governing party is “running out of time” to deliver change, in her most public criticism yet of the premier’s leadership.

item.title: Japan Tourism Rebounds as China Slump Made Up by Other Regions
item.description: Japan’s inbound tourism numbers returned to growth in February, as an increase from other regions made up for a continued slump in Chinese tourists.

item.title: Sumitomo Life Plans to Invest $1.9 Billion in Private Credit
item.description: Sumitomo Life Insurance Co. is considering allocating about ¥300 billion ($1.9 billion) on private credit in the fiscal year starting April, in the latest push by a Japanese financial firm into alternative assets to boost returns.

item.title: StanChart’s AI Chief Exiting Less Than a Year After Joining
item.description: The executive leading Standard Chartered Plc’s adoption of artificial intelligence is leaving the emerging markets lender less than a year into his role, according to people familiar with the matter.

item.title: China to Tap Vast Oil Stockpiles to Offset Iran Crisis, FGE Says
item.description: China, the world’s biggest crude importer, is close to tapping its vast commercial oil reserves as the Middle East war shows no signs of ending, according to FGE NexantECA.

item.title: Japan’s Gasoline Prices Surge to Record High on Iran War Impact
item.description: Japan’s gasoline prices surged to a record high this week, as ripple effects from the US-Israeli conflict with Iran reach Japan.

item.title: FTSE 100 Poised for Third Day of Gains
NSE Is Said to Set Modest Fee for Its $2.5 Billion India IPO
item.description: National Stock Exchange of India has set advisory fees at about 0.65% of the issue size for its upcoming initial public offering, according to people familiar with the matter.

item.title: Pimco Sees Private Credit Strains Triggering Wake-Up Call on Liquidity Risks
item.description: Mounting strains in the $1.8 trillion private credit market are giving investors a reality check and making them pay more attention to the illiquid nature of the asset class — and whether they are being adequately compensated for the risks, according to Pacific Investment Management Co.

item.title: Goldman Sees India Rupee Sliding to 95, Putting Pressure on RBI
item.description: India’s rupee may slump to 95 against the dollar over the next year because of the fallout from the Iran conflict, putting pressure on the central bank to act if inflation picks up, according to Goldman Sachs Group Inc.’s chief economist for the country.

item.title: Russian Oil Bound for China Is Diverted as India Grabs More
item.description: A Russian oil-laden tanker that was bound for China has U-turned in the South China Sea, and is now hurrying to India after New Delhi began doubling down on imports from Moscow.

item.title: EU Commission Chief to Visit Australia as Trade Deal Approaches
item.description: European Commission President Ursula von der Leyen will travel to Australia next week, Prime Minister Anthony Albanese announced, just days after she said that a trade deal between the two sides is “in the final stretch.”

item.title: Korea Stocks Extend Gains After Move to Ban Double Listings
item.description: South Korean stocks jumped after authorities moved to restrict publicly traded companies from listing certain subsidiaries, curbing a practice long blamed for diluting shareholder value.
